confirmed.title: Strategy token deadlocks on a dead/observer bot (whole population stops rating goals)
confirmed.portLocation: src/XonoticGodot.Server/Bot/BotBrain.cs:200-211 (dead/observer early-return) vs :246 (OnStrategyTokenUsed); BotPopulation.cs:429-453 (RotateStrategyToken gated only on _tokenTaken)
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/server/bot/default/havocbot/havocbot.qc:52-104 (token block, sets bot_strategytoken_taken=true at :103) vs the dead/frozen return at :113-119; bot.qc:791-813
confirmed.detail: In QC, havocbot_ai sets bot_strategytoken_taken = true at :103 UNCONDITIONALLY when the bot held the token — this runs BEFORE the dead/frozen return at :113. So even a dead or frozen token-holder consumes the token, and bot_serverframe (:791) rotates it to the next bot the following frame. The token therefore keeps cycling through corpses every frame and never stalls. In the port, BotBrain.ThinkProduce returns early for dead bots (:202-211) and observer bots (:200-201) BEFORE reaching OnStrategyTokenUsed at :246, so a dead/observer token-holder NEVER sets _tokenTaken. BotPopulation.RotateStrategyToken (BotPopulation.cs:433) only advances when _tokenTaken is true, so the token freezes on the corpse. RotateStrategyToken CAN land the token on a dead bot via its fallback `next = (_tokenIndex+1)%n` (BotPopulation.cs:439) whenever no live-goalless bot exists (common steady state — most bots have goals). Result: when the token lands on a dead bot, NO bot in the population runs its role / rates goals until that bot respawns (respawn delay can be several seconds), so bot navigation planning globally stalls intermittently. Observer bots are even worse: QC excludes observer/paused bots from bot_list entirely (bot.qc:437) so they never receive the token; the port keeps all brains in _brains and RotateStrategyToken excludes only IsDead (BotPopulation.cs:443), not IsObserver, so an observer bot can hold the token forever.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Invoke OnStrategyTokenUsed (set _tokenTaken=true) in the dead and observer early-return branches of ThinkProduce so a dead/observer holder still passes the token next frame, exactly as QC sets bot_strategytoken_taken=true at havocbot.qc:103 before the dead return. Alternatively (and matching QC more closely) exclude observer/paused bots from the token rotation set, mirroring bot_relinkplayerlist's bot_list membership (bot.qc:437).

verdict.title: Strategy token deadlocks on a dead/observer bot, stalling goal-rating for the whole bot population
verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED against Base QC. The token machine diverges from QC for dead and observer holders.

QC behavior (verified):
- bot.qc:62 bot_think for a DEAD bot does NOT return early — only IS_OBSERVER returns (bot.qc:137). The dead-handling block (bot.qc:130-149) falls through to bot.qc:160 `this.bot_ai(this)` (= havocbot_ai). Inside havocbot.qc:52, a dead token-holder enters the `if (bot_strategytoken == this && !bot_strategytoken_taken)` block (IS_DEAD is NOT part of the line-52 condition), skips havocbot_role (gated by FROZEN at :60 and the dead-return at :113-119), but reaches havocbot.qc:103 `bot_strategytoken_taken = true` UNCONDITIONALLY. So a dead QC bot consumes the token and bot_serverframe (bot.qc:791-813) rotates it next frame. No stall.
- An OBSERVER never gets the token in QC because bot_relinkplayerlist (bot.qc:437) excludes `IS_OBSERVER(it) || bot_ispaused(it)` from bot_list entirely, so the rotation loop (bot.qc:799-812) can never land bot_strategytoken on an observer.

Port behavior (verified):
- InputFor (BotPopulation.cs:180-193) calls brain.ThinkProduce once per tick for EVERY registered bot, with no dead/observer guard. Inside ThinkProduce (BotBrain.cs:168), the observer early-return at :200-201 and the dead early-return at :202-211 both occur BEFORE OnStrategyTokenUsed at :246. OnStrategyTokenUsed (wired at BotPopulation.cs:375 to `_tokenTaken = true`) is the ONLY writer of _tokenTaken=true (grep-confirmed; nothing re-arms it on death/respawn).
- RotateStrategyToken (BotPopulation.cs:429-453) only advances when _tokenTaken is true (:433). So a dead OR observer token-holder never sets _tokenTaken, the token freezes, and StrategyTokenHeld stays on that one brain — meaning NO bot in the population runs Role()/goal-rating until that bot becomes alive-and-active again. For the dead case this unsticks on respawn (a few seconds); for the observer case it can be permanent (RotateStrategyToken excludes only IsDead at :443, not IsObserver, and observers aren't pruned from _brains).
- The token CAN land on a dead bot: when no live-goalless bot exists (common steady state, since most live bots have goals), the live-bot search loop (:440-448) falls through to the fallback `next = (_tokenIndex+1)%n` (:439), which lands on the immediate next brain regardless of dead state. This matches QC's own land-on-anyone fallback, but QC tolerates it (dead holder still passes the token) whereas the port deadlocks.

The reviewer's one inaccurate sub-claim ("QC never lands the token on a corpse") doesn't matter: QC's rotation loop CAN land on a dead bot too (it breaks on bot_strategytoken_save==NULL when all are dead/have-goals), but QC is immune because the dead holder still sets bot_strategytoken_taken=true. The core diagnosis and the QC line citations are correct.

Severity major (not blocker): intermittent and self-healing for the dead case (token frees on respawn); the observer case is worse (can be permanent) but is an edge case in normal play. Net effect is degraded/stalled bot goal-planning, an AI-quality regression, not a crash. The suggested fix is correct and faithful to QC: set _tokenTaken=true in the dead/observer early-return branches (mirroring havocbot.qc:103) and/or exclude observers from RotateStrategyToken's rotation set (mirroring bot_relinkplayerlist's bot_list membership).

confirmed.title: Bots never use secondary fire — no per-weapon wr_aim dispatch
confirmed.portLocation: src/XonoticGodot.Server/Bot/BotBrain.cs:338-349 (Emit hard-codes ButtonAttack2=false) + :364-389 (AimAndDecideFire only ever returns a primary-fire decision)
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/server/bot/default/havocbot/havocbot.qc:149-159 (per-slot w.wr_aim(w,this,weaponentity) sets BOTH PHYS_INPUT_BUTTON_ATCK and PHYS_INPUT_BUTTON_ATCK2)
confirmed.detail: QC havocbot_ai loops over each weapon slot and calls the weapon's wr_aim, which is free to press secondary fire — devastator detonation, mortar bounce/secondary, vortex/charge, electro combos, etc. all rely on this. The port has no per-weapon wr_aim hook at all: BotBrain.AimAndDecideFire makes one generic primary-fire decision and Emit always emits ButtonAttack2=false. So bots fire only primary and, notably, never detonate a fired devastator rocket (wr_aim secondary) and never charge/release the vortex. The vortex-charge feature landed in T57 but bots can't drive it. This is a documented AI simplification (generic aim vs per-weapon wr_aim) rather than a crash, hence minor, but it is a visible behavioral gap a player notices.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add at least a minimal per-weapon secondary path for the weapons whose primary is useless without it (devastator detonation, mortar). Even a small ChosenWeapon-keyed switch that sets ButtonAttack2 under the right condition would close the worst gaps.

verdict.title: Bots never use secondary fire — no per-weapon wr_aim dispatch (ButtonAttack2 hardcoded false)
verdict.reasoning: Confirmed on both sides. Base QC havocbot.qc:149-159 loops over each weapon slot and calls w.wr_aim, which is free to set BOTH PHYS_INPUT_BUTTON_ATCK and PHYS_INPUT_BUTTON_ATCK2. The cited weapon proves the intent: Devastator wr_aim (Base devastator.qc:339-450) explicitly computes a detonate decision for skill>=2 and sets PHYS_INPUT_BUTTON_ATCK2(actor)=true (line 443), even forcing primary off when detonating (line 449). So stock bots demonstrably detonate guided rockets and drive other secondary mechanics. The port has no equivalent: BotBrain.AimAndDecideFire (BotBrain.cs:364-389) returns a single primary-fire bool, and Emit (BotBrain.cs:338-349) hardcodes ButtonAttack2=false. A project-wide grep confirms BotBrain.cs:348 is the ONLY place the bot path ever assigns ButtonAttack2. The secondary-fire machinery is real and wired to that button — WeaponFireDriver.Frame reads input.ButtonAttack2 (WeaponFireDriver.cs:58) and dispatches secondary for Devastator remote-detonate (Devastator.cs:126 RemoteDetonate), Electro combos, Crylink, Arc, Tuba, OkWeapons — but since the bot's MovementInput always carries false, none of it can ever fire for a bot. Net effect exactly as reviewed: bots fire primary only, never detonate a fired devastator rocket, never release a charged vortex (the T57 feature), never use mortar/electro/crylink secondaries. This is a behavioral/faithfulness gap, not a crash, and the BotBrain class header documents it as a deliberate simplification ('generic aim vs per-weapon wr_aim'), so minor is the right severity. Worth fixing because devastator (and mortar) secondaries are a visible AI-quality gap a player notices; a ChosenWeapon-keyed switch setting ButtonAttack2 for the worst-affected weapons would close it.

confirmed.title: bot_nofire cvar is silently ignored (bots fire even with bot_nofire 1)
confirmed.portLocation: src/XonoticGodot.Server/Bot/BotBrain.cs (no reference to bot_nofire anywhere; AimAndDecideFire :364-389 and ChooseEnemy :557-600 never check it)
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/server/bot/default/havocbot/havocbot.qc:142-146 (bot_nofire → clear ATCK/ATCK2) and :1336-1340 (bot_nofire → this.enemy = NULL)
confirmed.detail: QC gates firing on autocvar_bot_nofire in two places: havocbot_ai:142 zeroes both attack buttons, and havocbot_chooseenemy:1336-1340 nulls the enemy outright. bot_nofire is the standard cvar used while waypoint-editing/testing so bots roam but never shoot. The port honors bot_god (BotBrain.cs:182) but has no reference to bot_nofire at all, so `set bot_nofire 1` has no effect and bots keep firing. Silent drop of a QC behavior.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Guard the fire decision (AimAndDecideFire / Emit attack flag) and/or ChooseEnemy with Cvars.Bool("bot_nofire"), matching havocbot.qc:142 and :1336.

verdict.title: bot_nofire cvar is silently ignored (bots fire even with bot_nofire 1)
verdict.reasoning: Confirmed real. Base QC gates bot firing on autocvar_bot_nofire in both cited places: havocbot.qc:142-146 forces PHYS_INPUT_BUTTON_ATCK/ATCK2 false when set, and havocbot_chooseenemy (havocbot.qc:1336-1340) sets this.enemy = NULL and early-returns when set. The port (src/XonoticGodot.Server/Bot/BotBrain.cs) never reads bot_nofire: a project-wide grep finds only the cvar registration at Cvars.cs:225 (default "0", Save) and no read anywhere. AimAndDecideFire (BotBrain.cs:364-389) decides firing solely from aim cone + line of fire + Aim.ShouldFire, and ChooseEnemy (557-600) picks the nearest visible attackable enemy — neither checks bot_nofire. So `set bot_nofire 1` has no effect and bots keep firing, a silent drop of a stock QC behavior. The sibling diagnostic cvar bot_god IS honored at BotBrain.cs:182 via Cvars.Bool("bot_god"), proving the cvar-read pattern exists and is trivially applicable — bot_nofire was simply omitted. Severity minor: bot_nofire is a waypoint-editing/testing convenience cvar, not normal-play gameplay, but the fact it's registered with Save makes the no-op a mild trap. Reviewer's suggested fix (guard ChooseEnemy / the fire decision with Cvars.Bool("bot_nofire")) is accurate; nulling the enemy in ChooseEnemy alone suffices since AimAndDecideFire only runs with a non-null enemy, mirroring havocbot_chooseenemy:1336.

confirmed.title: Custom weapon-priority lists shipped empty; ChooseWeapon never reads them
confirmed.portLocation: src/XonoticGodot.Server/Cvars.cs:261-263 (priority_far/mid/close default "") + BotBrain.cs:443-490 (hand-rolled hitscan/splash heuristic, never reads the priority lists)
confirmed.baseRef: Base xonotic-server.cfg:155-157 ship non-empty ordered lists; qcsrc/server/bot/default/bot.qc:351-410 (bot_custom_weapon_priority_setup) + havocbot_chooseweapon consume them
confirmed.detail: Base xonotic-server.cfg sets bot_ai_custom_weapon_priority_far/mid/close to long non-empty ordered weapon lists (e.g. far = "vaporizer oknex vortex rifle electro devastator ..."), which bot_custom_weapon_priority_setup parses so havocbot_chooseweapon picks weapons by the configured priority per distance bucket. The port ships all three as "" in Cvars.cs (so even the QC code path would set bot_custom_weapon=false), and BotBrain.ChooseWeapon ignores them entirely — it uses a hand-rolled hitscan-far / splash-close / highest-impulse heuristic. Consequence: bot weapon selection ordering diverges from stock (e.g. the port keys on Weapon.Impulse, not the curated priority order, so it will not prefer vaporizer/vortex the way stock does). The distances cvar ("300 850") is read correctly; only the priority lists differ.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either ship the three priority lists with their Base values and have ChooseWeapon honor the per-distance ordered list (faithful), or explicitly document that the heuristic intentionally replaces them. At minimum the default values should match Base so a server admin's config behaves the same.

verdict.title: Bot custom weapon-priority lists shipped empty; ChooseWeapon ignores them, diverging from stock per-distance weapon ordering
verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed against both sides. BASE: xonotic-server.cfg:154-157 ships non-empty, curated, priority-ordered lists for far/mid/close (far = "vaporizer oknex vortex rifle electro devastator ..."). bot.qc:351-410 (bot_custom_weapon_priority_setup) parses them and, since stock's defaults are non-empty, sets bot_custom_weapon = true. havocbot.qc:1495-1607 (havocbot_chooseweapon) drives ALL in-combat weapon selection through these lists: the far/mid/close selection loops (lines 1568-1605) are entirely gated on `if(bot_custom_weapon)`, iterating bot_weapons_far/mid/close[] in priority order and picking the first owned weapon. So in stock the priority lists are the primary (not optional) weapon-selection mechanism. Note: with empty lists bot_custom_weapon stays false (bot.qc:361-366 early return), and in that state havocbot_chooseweapon does essentially nothing for an engaged enemy — so shipping "" is doubly non-faithful.

PORT: Cvars.cs:261-263 ships bot_ai_custom_weapon_priority_far/mid/close all as "" (only ..._distances "300 850" at line 260 matches Base). BotBrain.cs:443-490 (ChooseWeapon/PickOwned) never reads the three priority cvars at all; it buckets the range as far/mid/close (reading only the distances cvar at line 458/503), maps far→hitscan, close→splash via WeaponFlags.TypeHitscan/TypeSplash, then among candidates picks the highest w.Impulse (line 487). That ordering is unrelated to stock's curated lists, so bot weapon preference diverges from stock (e.g. stock prefers vaporizer/oknex/vortex by curated order; the port keys on Impulse). The reviewer's claims are all accurate. Severity minor: it is a bot-behavior fidelity gap, not a crash/correctness bug — the port still selects a usable weapon. Faithful fix is to ship the Base default lists and have ChooseWeapon honor the per-distance ordered list (parsing the cvar into a weapon-name priority order), or document the heuristic as an intentional replacement; at minimum the cvar defaults should match Base so an admin's server config behaves the same.

confirmed.title: Enemy-stick window and moving-target strategy interval not modeled
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/server/bot/default/havocbot/havocbot.qc:1350-1364 + :1468 (havocbot_stickenemy_time = time + bot_ai_enemydetectioninterval_stickingtoenemy [4s]); bot_ai_strategyinterval_movingtarget [5.5s] used when the goal can move
confirmed.detail: QC keeps a chosen enemy 'sticky' for bot_ai_enemydetectioninterval_stickingtoenemy (4s, halting target re-selection while the current enemy stays visible within 1000qu), and shortens the strategy re-rate to bot_ai_strategyinterval_movingtarget (5.5s) when the current goal is a moving entity. The port uses a flat 2s enemy re-evaluation and a flat 7s strategy interval, ignoring both _stickingtoenemy and _movingtarget. The cvars exist in Cvars.cs (lines 233, 235) but are dead — nothing reads them. Result: bots drop/re-pick targets more readily than stock and re-plan less responsively against carriers/moving items. Fidelity-only.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Implement the sticky-enemy window (extend the keep-current-enemy clock to bot_ai_enemydetectioninterval_stickingtoenemy with the visibility+<1000qu test) and use bot_ai_strategyinterval_movingtarget when Nav.GoalEntity is a movable entity (player/item).

verdict.title: Bot enemy-stick window and moving-target strategy interval not modeled
verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed against Base QC on both halves.

STICKY ENEMY: Base havocbot_chooseenemy (qcsrc/server/bot/default/havocbot/havocbot.qc:1342-1471) implements a sticky window: when a current enemy is still attackable AND havocbot_stickenemy_time is set and not expired (:1350), it traces visibility and tests vdist <1000qu (:1353-1356); if both pass it only defers re-pick by 0.5s (havocbot_chooseenemy_finished = time + 0.5) and returns early (:1359-1360); if the enemy is out of sight/range it clears sticky mode (:1364). On every full re-pick it stamps havocbot_stickenemy_time = time + autocvar_bot_ai_enemydetectioninterval_stickingtoenemy (:1468), which xonotic-server.cfg:140 defaults to 4. The port ChooseEnemy (src/XonoticGodot.Server/Bot/BotBrain.cs:557-600) merely keeps a still-attackable enemy and re-evaluates on the flat _chooseEnemyTime clock (EnemyDetectionInterval=2, BotBrain.cs:29,573). There is no stickenemy field, no 4s window, no visibility+1000qu test, and no 0.5s short re-check.

MOVING-TARGET STRATEGY: Base navigation_goalrating_timeout_set (qcsrc/server/bot/default/navigation.qc:20-26) sets the re-rate clock to autocvar_bot_ai_strategyinterval_movingtarget (5.5, xonotic-server.cfg:138) when IS_MOVABLE(goalentity), else autocvar_bot_ai_strategyinterval (7, :137). The port (BotBrain.cs:232) always reads bot_ai_strategyinterval (7) and never branches on whether Nav.GoalEntity is movable.

Both cvars are registered in the port (src/XonoticGodot.Server/Cvars.cs:233 strategyinterval_movingtarget, :235 enemydetectioninterval_stickingtoenemy) with correct defaults but are dead — no reader. So the port re-picks targets on a flat 2s clock instead of the 4s/0.5s sticky machine and re-plans at a flat 7s instead of 5.5s against movable goals.

Genuine divergence, correctly scoped as minor/fidelity-only: it changes bot AI feel (target persistence and re-plan responsiveness vs carriers/moving items), not correctness or the main play path; and per project memory BotBrain.Think isn't yet wired into the live --host loop, so user-facing impact today is nil.

confirmed.title: Danger check never honors hardwired-link / JUMP-waypoint commitment (committed:false hard-coded)
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/server/bot/default/havocbot/havocbot.qc:407-409 (gate excludes jumppadcount, waypoint_is_hardwiredlink, and goalcurrent_prev JUMP waypoints)
confirmed.detail: QC havocbot_checkdanger short-circuits (no danger braking) when the bot is on a jumppad (this.jumppadcount), traversing a hardwired link, or coming off a WAYPOINTFLAG_JUMP waypoint — i.e. a deliberate committed flight it must not brake out of. The port plumbs this through BotDanger.CheckDanger's `committed` parameter but the only live caller (BotBrain.cs:278) passes `committed: false` always, so a bot mid jump-pad arc or hardwired-link jump can still register 'danger ahead' over the gap below it and brake, aborting the intended jump. The parameter is documented as a simplification, but it means bots can fail jump-pad/gap traversals that QC handles. Fidelity-level.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Compute committed from the navigation state (Nav.LastTeleportTime / jumppad in progress, current link hardwired, or previous goal a JUMP waypoint) and pass it through, mirroring havocbot.qc:407-409.

verdict.title: Bot danger check hard-codes committed:false, ignoring jumppad/hardwired-link/JUMP-waypoint commitment
verdict.reasoning: Confirmed against both sides. Base QC havocbot.qc:401-409 (havocbot_checkdanger) returns 0/no-danger when the look-ahead traceline is clear AND none of three commitment conditions hold: this.jumppadcount (on a jumppad arc), waypoint_is_hardwiredlink(goalcurrent_prev, goalcurrent) (map-maker hardwired link, helper at waypoints.qc:283-298), or goalcurrent_prev being a WAYPOINTFLAG_JUMP waypoint — i.e. a deliberate committed flight the bot must not brake out of. The port plumbs this correctly: BotDanger.CheckDanger (src/XonoticGodot.Server/Bot/BotDanger.cs:40) implements the gate as `if (ahead.Fraction < 1f || committed) return 0;`, mirroring the QC short-circuit. But the only live caller, BotBrain.cs:277-278, passes `committed: false` unconditionally (with an inline comment at BotDanger.cs:29 documenting it as a known simplification). The CheckDanger result in BotBrain.cs:293-305 is an INDEPENDENT second brake that overrides `move` with `-bot.Velocity` when danger is seen — separate from Steer's own inline ledge-brake (BotNavigation.cs:267-279), which DOES honor a `committing` flag (line 270: Teleport|Jump goals + ladders). So a bot mid jumppad/JUMP/hardwired-link flight can have Steer correctly commit, yet the CheckDanger path independently detect 'void/cliff/trigger_hurt below the look-ahead point' over the gap it is leaping and brake out of the intended jump — exactly the case the QC gate exists to prevent. The needed state is all available in the port (Nav.WantJump / WaypointFlags.Jump for JUMP waypoints, Nav.LastTeleportTime for jumppad/teleport-in-progress, LoadHardwiredLinks/Waypoint net for hardwired links; Steer already derives an equivalent `committing` flag), so the fix is to compute `committed` from navigation state and pass it through. The finding's mechanism and Base citation are accurate. Severity is minor (not major): this affects only bot-AI navigation quality, not a player-facing correctness system; bots are skill-gated and degrade gracefully; and Steer's inline brake already commits correctly, so the failure window is the narrow case where the redundant CheckDanger brake fires mid-arc. The suggested fix is correct in spirit, though the precise QC mapping is goalcurrent_prev-based (the PREVIOUS goal was a JUMP waypoint / the current link is hardwired), not just LastTeleportTime.

confirmed.title: accuracy_byte uses banker's rounding (ToEven) instead of DP rint's round-half-up
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/server/weapons/accuracy.qc:23 (1 + rint(n*100.0/d)); darkplaces/prvm_cmds.c:1508-1518 (VM_rint)
confirmed.detail: QC accuracy_byte returns 1 + rint(n*100/d). DP's rint (VM_rint) is floor(f+0.5) for f>0, i.e. round-half-AWAY-from-zero. Since n,d are non-negative the argument is >= 0, so DP rounds exact halves UP. The port computes `1 + (int)System.MathF.Round(n*100f/d)`, and MathF.Round defaults to MidpointRounding.ToEven (banker's rounding). At exact half-integer percentages the two disagree by 1: e.g. n=101,d=200 -> n*100/d = 50.5; DP -> 1+51 = 52 (51%), port -> 1+50 = 51 (50%). The in-code comment ('rint rounds half-to-even like the DP builtin') is factually wrong — DP rint is round-half-up, not half-to-even. This shifts the networked accuracy byte and the displayed accuracy% by 1 at any .5 boundary. No test pins the value, so the fix is safe.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace with the DP rint for non-negative input: `return 1 + (int)System.MathF.Floor(n * 100f / d + 0.5f);` and correct the doc comment to 'round half away from zero (DP rint)'.

verdict.title: accuracy_byte uses banker's rounding (ToEven) instead of DP rint's round-half-away-from-zero
verdict.reasoning: Confirmed against both sides. Base QC accuracy.qc:23 returns `1 + rint(n * 100.0 / d)`. DP's VM_rint (Base/darkplaces/prvm_cmds.c:1508-1518) is `floor(f + 0.5)` for f>0 and `ceil(f - 0.5)` otherwise — i.e. round-half-AWAY-from-zero. Since n,d are non-negative the argument is >= 0, so DP rounds exact halves UP. The port (src/XonoticGodot.Common/Gameplay/Weapons/WeaponAccuracyEvents.cs:133) does `1 + (int)System.MathF.Round(n * 100f / d)`, and MathF.Round with no MidpointRounding argument defaults to ToEven (banker's rounding). I compiled and ran both: MathF.Round(50.5f)=50 (rounds to even), and for n=101,d=200 (arg=50.5) the port yields byte 51 while DP yields 52; for n=1,d=200 (arg=0.5) port=1 vs DP=2 — both DIVERGE exactly as the reviewer described. The in-code comment at line 127 ('rint rounds half-to-even like the DP builtin') is factually inverted. This is the live wire path: Scores.AccuracyBytes (Scores.cs:407-414) feeds ServerNet.cs:1297 which networks the byte to clients, and it also gates the dirty/SendFlags check at Scores.cs:399 (before != AccuracyByte(...)), so the off-by-one can additionally shift when an accuracy update is flagged for transmission. No test in tests/ references AccuracyByte/accuracy_byte, so the value is unpinned and the fix is safe. Suggested fix is correct: `return 1 + (int)System.MathF.Floor(n * 100f / d + 0.5f);` (valid because the argument is always non-negative here) and correct the doc comment to 'round half away from zero (DP rint)'. Severity is minor: faithful-port defect with a bounded ±1 effect only at exact .5% boundaries, cosmetic in practice.

confirmed.title: Vortex overcharge sound plays at fixed volume; QC scales it by charge
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/common/weapons/weapon/vortex.qc:138-139
confirmed.detail: In W_Vortex_Attack the overcharge zap is emitted with a volume that ramps with how overcharged the shot is: sound(actor, CH_WEAPON_B, SND_VORTEX_CHARGE, VOL_BASE * (charge - 0.5*charge_animlimit) / (1 - 0.5*charge_animlimit), ATTN_NORM). The port plays Api.Sound.Play(actor, SoundChannel.Body, "weapons/nexcharge.wav") at the default (full) volume, dropping the (charge - 0.5*animlimit)/(1 - 0.5*animlimit) scaling. The trigger condition (charge_animlimit > 0 && charge > charge_animlimit) is correct; only the volume envelope is missing, so a barely-overcharged shot is as loud as a fully-overcharged one. Cosmetic.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Pass a scaled volume to the play call: vol = VOL_BASE * (charge - 0.5f*Cvars.ChargeAnimLimit) / (1f - 0.5f*Cvars.ChargeAnimLimit), if the sound API accepts a volume arg (as the impact/fire paths elsewhere do).

verdict.title: Vortex overcharge sound plays at fixed full volume; QC scales it by charge level
verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading both sides. Base QC W_Vortex_Attack (qcsrc/common/weapons/weapon/vortex.qc:138-139) emits the overcharge zap with a charge-scaled volume: sound(actor, CH_WEAPON_B, SND_VORTEX_CHARGE, VOL_BASE * (charge - 0.5*charge_animlimit) / (1 - 0.5*charge_animlimit), ATTN_NORM). The port (Vortex.cs:272-273) calls Api.Sound.Play(actor, SoundChannel.Body, "weapons/nexcharge.wav") with no volume argument. The ISoundService.Play signature (Services.cs:112: `void Play(Entity e, SoundChannel channel, string sample, float volume = 1f, float attenuation = 1f, ...)`) defaults volume to 1f, so every overcharge shot plays at volume 1.0 regardless of charge. The trigger condition (ChargeAnimLimit>0 && charge>ChargeAnimLimit) matches QC exactly; only the volume envelope is dropped. With stock VOL_BASE=0.7 (GameSound.cs:57) and charge_animlimit=0.5 (Vortex.cs:86), a barely-overcharged shot should be ~0.23 and a fully-overcharged one ~0.7 — the port makes both a flat 1.0, so the loudness ramp is lost AND the peak is louder than QC. The suggested fix is viable: Cvars.ChargeAnimLimit is a real field (Vortex.cs:36) and Play accepts a volume arg. Minor refinement: also pass attenuation: SoundLevels.AttenNorm explicitly, since Play's default attenuation is 1f and QC uses ATTN_NORM. Genuine but cosmetic — correctly classified minor.

confirmed.title: T48 content keys are NOT plumbed on the live --host path (GameWorld.ApplyDictFields never calls MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply)
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/common/mapobjects/{misc/laser.qc, models.qc, func/pointparticles.qc, func/rainsnow.qc, target/music.qc} — DP ED_ParseEdict copies EVERY map key onto the edict
confirmed.detail: GameDemo.ApplyMapFields calls MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply (GameDemo.cs:997), but the LIVE play-path twin GameWorld.ApplyDictFields (used by NetGame, the real --host / Create-match path) does NOT. ApplyDictFields only promotes dmg/noise/volume/atten/cnt/count/model/spawnflags/speed/wait/lip/height — it never promotes the T48-specific keys: mdl, beam_color, colormod, alpha, scale, modelscale, solid, fade_start/fade_end, alpha_max/alpha_min, fade_vertical_offset, velocity, movedir, mins, maxs, lifetime, fade_time, fade_rate, waterlevel(jitter), impulse. Consequence on the live path: (1) func_pointparticles is FEATURE-DEAD — its entire effect identity is .mdl (PointParticles.cs reads e.Mdl; MapParticleEmitters.Build resolves the effectinfo block from e.Mdl), so with mdl unset the emitter renders nothing and impulse/velocity/movedir/mins-maxs are all default; (2) misc_laser renders with wrong cosmetics — beam_color defaults to red, scale=1, modelscale=1, mdl='' regardless of map keys (the laser still DAMAGES because dmg IS plumbed); (3) func_rain/func_snow lose custom fall direction (velocity unplumbed → Dest falls back to default -700/-300); (4) func_wall/func_static/misc_gamemodel lose solid override, scale, modelscale and all distance-fade keys; (5) target_music/trigger_music lose lifetime/fade_time/fade_rate (music still plays since noise+volume are plumbed, but the lifetime priority window and fades are gone). The MapObjectFieldsExtra.cs docstring even asserts 'the shared helper both spawn paths call (GameWorld.ApplyDictFields and GameDemo.ApplyMapFields)' — that contract is broken: GameWorld does not call it.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add `XonoticGodot.Common.Gameplay.MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply(e, f);` in GameWorld.ApplyDictFields (before the final SetOrigin at line 1987), exactly as GameDemo.cs:997 does. The helper is idempotent on the keys GameWorld already promotes (dmg/volume/atten/noise/cnt/count), so re-parsing them is safe.

verdict.title: T48 content keys not plumbed on live --host path: GameWorld.ApplyDictFields never calls MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply
verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED against both sides.

Port evidence:
- GameDemo.ApplyMapFields calls XonoticGodot.Common.Gameplay.MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply(e, f) at game/GameDemo.cs:997.
- GameWorld.ApplyDictFields (src/XonoticGodot.Server/GameWorld.cs:1904-1989) promotes only a fixed key set (angle/targetname/target/killtarget/message/model/spawnflags/team/skin/spawnmob/count/monster_moveflags/cnt/health/dmg/noalign/monster_skill/noise/noise1-3/volume/atten/speed/wait/lip/height) and NEVER calls MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply. A repo-wide grep finds MapObjectFieldsExtra referenced in exactly 3 files: game/GameDemo.cs (the call site), the helper's own definition file, and a planning brief — zero references anywhere in src/XonoticGodot.Server/. So GameWorld genuinely does not invoke it.
- The helper's docstring (MapObjectFieldsExtra.cs:86-90) asserts it is 'the shared helper both spawn paths call (GameWorld.ApplyDictFields and GameDemo.ApplyMapFields)' — that contract is factually broken.
- GameWorld is the live play path: NetGame.StartListenServer does new GameWorld(collision, mapEntities) (game/net/NetGame.cs:417) for the --host / Create-match listen server, and GameWorld.SpawnMapEntities (GameWorld.cs:1829) calls ApplyDictFields(e, dict) at line 1856 before dispatching each spawnfunc. This is the real Create-match/--host path, not dead code.

Consumer evidence (these fields are load-bearing and read only from the Entity, so on the live path they stay default):
- PointParticles.PointParticlesSetup reads this_.Mdl, this_.Impulse, this_.ParticleCount, this_.Mins/Maxs, this_.Velocity (src/XonoticGodot.Common/Gameplay/MapObjects/PointParticles.cs); the client emitter MapParticleEmitters resolves the effectinfo block from Entity.Mdl. With mdl/impulse/count/velocity/mins/maxs all unpopulated on the live path, func_pointparticles emits nothing → feature-dead.
- Laser reads BeamColor/ColorModKey/ScaleFactor/ModelScale/AlphaKey/Mdl; on the live path these all stay default (beam_color zero, scale/modelscale 0) → misc_laser renders with wrong cosmetics (it still DAMAGES because dmg IS plumbed in ApplyDictFields).
- func_rain/func_snow custom fall direction (velocity→Dest), func_wall/func_static distance-fade keys, and target_music lifetime/fade_time/fade_rate are likewise unplumbed.

Base evidence (DP ED_ParseEdict generically copies EVERY map key onto the edict; the port replaces that with the fixed promotion + this helper):
- qcsrc/common/mapobjects/func/pointparticles.qc reads this.mdl (line 97/113/142), this.impulse (105-106), this.count, this.velocity/movedir (15,57-58), this.mins/maxs (19,41-50,110-111).
- qcsrc/common/mapobjects/misc/laser.qc reads beam_color (142-144,178,238-244), legacy colormod aliasing (238-241), this.scale/modelscale (131,149-150), this.alpha (129,146), this.mdl/cnt (217-235). All present exactly as cited.

Severity: rated MAJOR rather than blocker — it degrades visual/effect fidelity on a path users actually hit (func_pointparticles dead, misc_laser mis-tinted, rain/snow/music fades lost) but does not crash or break core gameplay (laser still damages via plumbed dmg; music still plays via plumbed noise/volume). The reviewer's suggested one-line fix is correct: add MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply(e, f) in ApplyDictFields before the final SetOrigin; the helper is idempotent on the dmg/volume/atten/noise/cnt/count keys GameWorld already promotes, and it writes colormod into a separate ColorModKey field to avoid clashing with any render-side colormod promotion.

confirmed.title: func_pointparticles emitters live but inert on --host (mdl never set → no particle effect)
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/common/mapobjects/func/pointparticles.qc:53-54,97 (WriteString mdl; cnt=0 'use a good handler') and Draw_PointParticles:204-217 (_particleeffectnum(this.mdl))
confirmed.detail: MapParticleEmitters is hosted by ClientWorld on the live path (ClientWorld.cs:318), and Rescan/Build run against the facade, but Build resolves the effectinfo block ONLY from e.Mdl (Effects.GetInfoBlocks(e.Mdl)). Because the live GameWorld path never sets e.Mdl (finding #1), every map-placed func_pointparticles falls into the no-block branch and emits the generic default spark mesh with rate from e.Impulse — but e.Impulse is also unset (defaults to 1 via the spawnfunc), so a map's intended effect (e.g. a smoke/fire/fountain effectinfo) does not appear at all. This is the visible-to-player consequence of #1 isolated for the pointparticles family. func_sparks is unaffected because SparksSetup hardcodes Mdl='TE_SPARK' in the spawnfunc itself (PointParticles.cs:91), not from the dict.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Fixed transitively by finding #1 (plumb mdl/impulse/velocity/movedir/mins/maxs/waterlevel into GameWorld.ApplyDictFields). No separate change needed once Apply is wired.

verdict.title: func_pointparticles emitters render a wrong generic spark instead of the map's named effect on --host (e.Mdl never set)
verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both sides plus the shipped map data.

BASE QC (qcsrc/common/mapobjects/func/pointparticles.qc): the SVQC spawnfunc nets this.mdl (WriteString MSG_ENTITY this.mdl, line 54) and sets cnt=0 'use a good handler' when mdl is set (line 97). The CSQC Draw_PointParticles resolves the effect from the name when cnt==0: eff_num = _particleeffectnum(this.mdl) (lines 207, 216). So the map-placed mdl string is the source of the rendered effect.

PORT: MapParticleEmitters.Build resolves the effectinfo block ONLY from e.Mdl — Effects.GetInfoBlocks(e.Mdl) (MapParticleEmitters.cs:134). It is hosted by ClientWorld on the live listen-server/--host path (ClientWorld.cs:318), and the GameWorld spawn path runs the spawnfunc via SpawnFuncs.TrySpawn (GameWorld.cs:1861).

ROOT CAUSE (the cited finding #1): the mdl/impulse/velocity/movedir/mins/maxs/count keys are promoted by MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply (MapObjectFieldsExtra.cs:102-116), but that helper is called ONLY from the demo path (GameDemo.cs:997). The live GameWorld.ApplyDictFields (GameWorld.cs:1904-1989) hand-promotes a fixed key set (targetname/target/model/spawnflags/team/skin/count→Count(int)/cnt/health/dmg/noise/volume/atten/speed/wait/lip/height) and never calls MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply, so e.Mdl/e.Impulse/e.ParticleJitter/e.MoveDir/e.Velocity stay at defaults on --host. The T48 brief (planning/wave-a3/briefs/T48.json:31) explicitly required adding the GameWorld.ApplyDictFields one-line hook; grep shows MapObjectFieldsExtra is referenced only at GameDemo.cs:997 — the GameWorld hook was never added.

CONSEQUENCE: with e.Mdl=="" the no-block branch is taken, so the emitter renders the generic warm-spark DefaultSparkMesh (MapParticleEmitters.cs:247,273) instead of the map's effect. e.Impulse/e.ParticleCount are NOT zero (the spawnfunc PointParticlesSetup defaults them to 1, PointParticles.cs:50-53), so the emitter is not fully inert — it dribbles a wrong generic spark at ~1 emission/sec rather than the intended effect. The finding's headline 'no particle effect' overstates this slightly (it is wrong-effect, not zero-effect), but the substance — the map's intended effect does not appear — is correct.

REAL-WORLD IMPACT (verified against assets/data/xonotic-20230620-maps.pk3 BSP entity lumps): 6 shipped maps place func_pointparticles with named mdl + explicit impulse/velocity/count: courtfun (mdl "steam"/"smoking", impulse 50/2/10/4), stormkeep (mdl "sparks", impulse 4, count 6, velocity "50 -100 30"), bromine (mdl "map_bromine_pipe_smoke", impulse 5), techassault (mdl "TE_EXPLOSION", impulse 3, ×many). All of these lose their intended effect on --host. func_sparks is correctly unaffected because SparksSetup hardcodes this_.Mdl="TE_SPARK" in the spawnfunc itself (PointParticles.cs:91), independent of the dict.

FIX: as the finding says, this is fixed transitively by finding #1 — add the MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply(e, f) one-liner to GameWorld.ApplyDictFields (mirroring GameDemo.cs:997). No separate change needed for the pointparticles family once that hook is wired. Major (player-visible loss of intended map ambience across multiple stock maps), not blocker (no crash/sim divergence; func_sparks and the demo path still work).

confirmed.title: target_music/trigger_music lose lifetime + fade_time/fade_rate on the live path
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/common/mapobjects/target/music.qc:31-40 (target_music_sendto writes volume/fade_time/fade_rate/lifetime) and TargetMusic_Advance:182-201 (fade ramps by frametime/fade_time and frametime/fade_rate; music_target outranks default only while time < lifetime)
confirmed.detail: On the live path these three keys are never promoted (finding #1), so MusicPlayer.EvaluateMusicSources always sees MusicLifetime<=0 (the triggered-target branch at MusicPlayer.cs:171 short-circuits, treating EVERY used targeted target_music as a lifetime-0 default-slot claim) and Fades() always returns DefaultFadeTime instead of the map's fade_time/fade_rate. Net effect: a triggered target_music that should temporarily override the default for `lifetime` seconds either never enters the timed-override branch or never expires; crossfades use the generic 2 s default rather than the map's authored fades. Music still plays (noise+volume are plumbed by GameWorld), so this is fidelity, not feature-death. Note also the port's own documented deviation (MusicPlayer.cs:226-231): QC fade_time/fade_rate of 0 means INSTANT, but the port substitutes DefaultFadeTime — divergent for any map that intentionally sets fade 0.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Resolved by finding #1 (plumb lifetime/fade_time/fade_rate). Separately, decide whether to honor QC's fade==0 'instant' semantics rather than overriding with DefaultFadeTime.

verdict.title: target_music/trigger_music lose lifetime + fade_time/fade_rate on the live (NetGame/GameWorld) path
verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED. Root cause (the finding's "finding #1") verified directly: GameWorld.ApplyDictFields (src/XonoticGodot.Server/GameWorld.cs:1904-1989) is the live --host/NetGame server spawn path, and it promotes only a fixed key set (targetname/target/model/noise/volume/atten/speed/wait/lip/height/etc., ending at line 1985) and never calls MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply. That helper (src/.../MapObjects/MapObjectFieldsExtra.cs:134-136) is the ONLY writer of e.MusicLifetime/MusicFadeIn/MusicFadeOut, and a Grep shows it is invoked only from GameDemo.cs:997, never from the server path. NetGame is the live path: it builds the GameWorld (NetGame.cs:417) and points MusicPlayer.EntityList straight at _serverWorld.Services.EntityTable.All (NetGame.cs:755). So on the live path those three fields stay 0.

Downstream effects confirmed in game/client/MusicPlayer.cs: line 171 `if (e.MusicLifetime <= 0f) continue;` short-circuits the timed-override branch for every triggered target_music, and the default-slot pass (lines 194-205) then treats a triggered targeted track (MusicActivationTime>=0, MusicLifetime==0) as a PERMANENT default-slot claim that never expires. Fades() (lines 229-231) always returns (DefaultFadeTime, DefaultFadeTime) because MusicFadeIn/MusicFadeOut are 0, so crossfades ignore the map's authored fade_time/fade_rate.

Base QC clearly contradicts the port: target_music_sendto (music.qc:36-38) transmits fade_time/fade_rate/lifetime; Net_TargetMusic (music.qc:252-270) branches on `tim` to set music_target with `e.lifetime = time + tim` (tim>0) vs music_default (tim==0); TargetMusic_Advance (music.qc:182-201) outranks the default only while `time < music_target.lifetime` and ramps volume by frametime/fade_time (up) and frametime/fade_rate (down). entities.ent documents fade_time/fade_rate/lifetime as real mapper keys for target_music (and fade_time/fade_rate for trigger_music). The port can never reproduce a temporary, expiring override or the authored fades on the live path.

Secondary point also valid: QC's fade==0 means INSTANT (music.qc:195 `(it.fade_time > 0) ? bound(...) : 1`, music.qc:200 `(it.fade_rate > 0) ? bound(...) : 0`), but the port substitutes DefaultFadeTime (MusicPlayer.cs:230-231) — a real, self-documented deviation that matters once the keys are plumbed.

Severity minor: music still plays via the cdtrack/default path (noise+volume are plumbed by GameWorld), so this is fidelity loss (lifetime override window + authored crossfade timing) on maps that author these keys, not feature-death — matching the reviewer's own scoping. Fix is exactly as suggested: add the three music keys to GameWorld.ApplyDictFields (or have it call MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply like GameDemo does), and separately decide whether to honor fade==0 instant semantics instead of DefaultFadeTime.

confirmed.title: func_rain/func_snow custom fall direction (velocity/wind) lost on --host
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/common/mapobjects/func/rainsnow.qc:33-36,73-76 (this.dest = this.velocity; if(!this.dest) this.dest = '0 0 -700'/'0 0 -300')
confirmed.detail: RainSetup/SnowSetup move .velocity onto .Dest as the fall direction. On the live path velocity is never promoted (finding #1) so Dest is always the default straight-down vector, dropping any map-authored wind (X/Y velocity) or custom fall speed. Low impact because the recon confirms no shipped stock BSP uses func_rain/func_snow, but it is a real divergence for custom maps. cnt (palette) and count (density) ARE plumbed by GameWorld so color/density are correct.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Resolved by finding #1 (plumb velocity).

verdict.title: func_rain/func_snow custom fall direction (wind) lost on the live --host path
verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED — real divergence, downstream of finding #1.

Base QC (rainsnow.qc:33-36, 73-76): spawnfunc does `this.dest = this.velocity; this.velocity = '0 0 0'; if(!this.dest) this.dest = '0 0 -700'` (snow '0 0 -300'). The QUAKED docs (lines 24-25, 64-65) state the velocity key is the falling direction and "use the X and Y velocity for wind". rainsnow_SendEntity:13 networks `this.dest` to the client; the CSQC handler (:126) reads it into .velocity and feeds te_particlerain/te_particlesnow (:115-117) as the per-particle fall+wind vector. So map-authored X/Y velocity = wind, and a non-default Z = custom fall speed — both intended features.

Port faithfully mirrors the spawnfunc: RainSnow.cs:41-44 does `this_.Dest = this_.Velocity; ... if (this_.Dest == Vector3.Zero) this_.Dest = defaultDest;` and WeatherSystem.cs:100 reads `e.Dest` as the renderer's velocity. The logic at both ends is correct.

The break is the input: e.Velocity is never populated from the `velocity` map key on the live host path. The `velocity` key parse lives in MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply (MapObjectFieldsExtra.cs:113, `if (TryVec(fields,"velocity",...)) e.Velocity = vel`), but Apply is invoked ONLY from GameDemo.cs:997 (ApplyMapFields). The live --host server path is GameWorld.SpawnMapEntities → ApplyDictFields (GameWorld.cs:1856, 1904-1989), which sets origin/angles/target/team/skin/count/cnt/health/dmg/noise/speed/wait/lip/height etc. but NEVER calls MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply and NEVER parses `velocity`. So on --host, e.Velocity stays Vector3.Zero → RainSetup makes Dest=Zero → the `if (Dest==Zero)` branch forces the straight-down default, discarding any map wind/custom fall speed. This is exactly finding #1's seam, and this is a genuine downstream consequence.

Two precisions on the finding's framing: (1) the suggested fix (plumb velocity into ApplyDictFields, or call MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply from it) does resolve it. (2) The finding says "count (density) ARE plumbed by GameWorld so density is correct" — partially off: ApplyDictFields:1941 sets e.Count (int) but RainSnow.Setup/WeatherSystem read e.ParticleCount (the float at MapObjectFieldsExtra.cs:27), which is ALSO only set in Apply:105-109. So on --host, ParticleCount is 0 too and falls to the 2000 default — i.e. a custom `count` density is ALSO silently lost on the host path, same root cause. Cnt (palette) IS plumbed (ApplyDictFields:1948).

Severity minor is right: no shipped stock BSP uses func_rain/func_snow (the port's own headers note the 31-BSP recon census, RainSnow.cs:8-9 and WeatherSystem.cs:14-15), so zero impact on stock maps; affects only custom maps, and even then it's a visual-only weather nicety.

confirmed.title: func_wall/func_static solid override + distance-fade keys unplumbed on --host
confirmed.baseRef: qcsrc/common/mapobjects/models.qc:178-179 (if(!ent.solid) ent.solid=sol; else if(ent.solid<0) ent.solid=SOLID_NOT) and Ent_Wall_PreDraw:236-252 (fade_start/fade_end/alpha_max/alpha_min distance fade)
confirmed.detail: GModelInit branches on ent.SolidOverride to honor a mapper `solid` key (e.g. a func_wall a map deliberately makes non-solid, or a func_illusionary forced solid). On the live path `solid` is never promoted (finding #1) so SolidOverride is always 0 and the family default is always used — a map that overrides solidity per-entity is silently ignored. Likewise fade_start/fade_end/alpha_max/alpha_min/fade_vertical_offset are unplumbed, so the distance-fade the ENT_CLIENT_WALL renderer implements never triggers (props that should fade out at range stay fully opaque, or vice-versa). The header comment in MapModels.cs claims the fade keys 'ride the entity for it', but on the live path they do not.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Resolved by finding #1 (plumb solid/scale/modelscale/fade_*).

verdict.title: func_wall/func_static solid override + distance-fade keys unplumbed on --host path
verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED (with one correction to the finding's framing).

Structural facts verified by reading both sides:

1) Base QC g_model_init (Base/.../qcsrc/common/mapobjects/models.qc:178-179): `if(!ent.solid) ent.solid=(sol); else if(ent.solid<0) ent.solid=SOLID_NOT;` — a mapper `solid` key per-entity overrides the family default. The port reproduces this branch in MapModels.cs:71-76 (GModelInit), keyed off ent.SolidOverride.

2) But SolidOverride is only ever set by MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply (MapObjectFieldsExtra.cs:126). That helper is called ONLY from GameDemo.cs:997 (demo-playback path). The live --host server path is GameWorld.SpawnMapEntities -> ApplyDictFields (GameWorld.cs:1856,1904-1989): I read all of ApplyDictFields and it promotes targetname/target/model/spawnflags/team/skin/count/cnt/health/dmg/noise*/volume/atten/speed/wait/lip/height/... but NOT `solid`, `scale`, `modelscale`, or any `fade_*` key, and it never calls MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply. So on --host, ent.SolidOverride is always 0, ent.ScaleFactor/ModelScale always 0, and GModelInit always falls through to the family default solidity. A map that sets `solid` on a func_wall/func_static/func_illusionary is silently ignored on the live server. This is a genuine divergence from g_model_init.

CORRECTION to the finding: the finding says the plumbing gap means "the distance-fade the ENT_CLIENT_WALL renderer implements never triggers." There is NO such renderer in the port. MapModels.cs:11-14 explicitly declares the ENT_CLIENT_WALL Net_LinkEntity stream + Ent_Wall_PreDraw distance-fade (Base models.qc:224-259) OUT OF SCOPE. I grepped for consumers of Entity.FadeStartDist/FadeEndDist/AlphaMax/AlphaMin/FadeVerticalOffset: the only reads are EffectInfo/EffectInfoParticle (unrelated particle fields) and WeatherSystem.cs:165 (rain/snow draw distance). No func_wall prop renderer reads them. So even if the fade_* keys were plumbed on --host, nothing would fade — the fade half of this finding is inert regardless of plumbing. The header comment's claim that the fade keys "ride the entity for it" is aspirational, but the absence of a wall renderer (not the missing promotion) is why fade does nothing.

Net: the `solid` override gap is a real, faithful-to-QC divergence on the live path and worth fixing (cheaply, by calling MapObjectFieldsExtra.Apply from GameWorld.ApplyDictFields, which also resolves finding #1). The fade-keys gap is real-as-plumbing but functionally moot until a wall renderer exists. Severity minor is correct: it only bites maps that override per-entity solidity on these brush families (uncommon — the family defaults already cover func_wall=solid / func_illusionary=non-solid), and it does not affect the world BSP collision that makes func_walls block (that comes from SetBrushEntityModel + Solid.Bsp, which IS the unconditional family default). No movement/combat/security impact.

confirmed.title: FreezeTag gametype-rules freeze (NULL attacker) wrongly deducts a point from the victim
confirmed.baseRef: common/gametypes/gametype/freezetag/sv_freezetag.qc:162-181 (freezetag_Add_Score)
confirmed.detail: Base freezetag_Add_Score gives NO score when the attacker is NULL (frozen by the gametype rules themselves — e.g. late-spawn freeze in PlayerSpawn/freezetag_Freeze(player, NULL), or the autoteamchange path). Its branches are: attacker==targ -> targ -1; else IS_PLAYER(attacker) -> ±1 + targ -1; ELSE (NULL/non-player) -> nothing (the trailing comment 'else nothing - got frozen by the gametype rules themselves'). The port folds attacker==null INTO the self-freeze branch (`if (attacker is null || ReferenceEquals(attacker, targ)) targ.ScoreFrags -= 1`), so a rules-freeze with NULL attacker incorrectly costs the victim 1 SCORE where Base costs 0. This affects the SP_SCORE that feeds the scoreboard (and thus the T53 scoreboard block), though not the team round-win condition. Observable: a player frozen on a late join loses a scoreboard point in the port but not in stock.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Split the null case from the self-freeze case: `if (attacker is null) { /* gametype-rules freeze: no score */ } else if (ReferenceEquals(attacker, targ)) targ.ScoreFrags -= 1; else if (SameTeam) {...} else {...}`.

verdict.title: FreezeTag rules-freeze (NULL attacker) wrongly deducts a point from the victim
verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both sides. Base freezetag_Add_Score (sv_freezetag.qc:162-181) has THREE branches: (1) attacker==targ -> targ SCORE -1; (2) IS_PLAYER(attacker) -> attacker +1/-1 and targ SCORE -1; (3) else (NULL/non-player) -> NOTHING, with the explicit trailing comment "else nothing - got frozen by the gametype rules themselves". The NULL-attacker paths are real and reachable: freezetag_Freeze(player, NULL) is called from the ft PlayerSpawn hook at line 541 (player was dead, frozen_timeout <= -2) and line 551 (late join during an active round, after CENTER_FREEZETAG_SPAWN_LATE). In both, Base applies no score change.

The port (FreezeTag.cs:237-241) collapses null INTO the self-freeze branch: `if (attacker is null || ReferenceEquals(attacker, targ)) targ.ScoreFrags -= 1;`. So a rules-freeze with a NULL attacker costs the victim 1 SCORE in the port where Base costs 0 — a clear contradiction with the cited Base source. This affects SP_SCORE (scoreboard) only, not the team round-win condition, so minor is correct.

Caveat on materiality: in the port, Freeze() currently has a single caller, OnDeath (line 208), passing `ev.Attacker as Player`; this is null on non-player/world/suicide deaths, so the buggy branch IS reachable today (those deaths would now also wrongly self-penalize unless they go through the attacker==targ path anyway). Even where latent, the score matrix in this public method is wrong relative to freezetag_Add_Score, so the fix stands. Suggested fix (split `if (attacker is null) { /* no score */ } else if (ReferenceEquals(attacker, targ)) ...`) is correct.

confirmed.title: Survival hunter-count + assignment use the full roster (incl. dead/observers) and roster order, not live-player count / random pick
confirmed.baseRef: common/gametypes/gametype/survival/sv_survival.qc:160-193 (Surv_RoundStart)
confirmed.detail: Base Surv_RoundStart computes playercount from FOREACH_CLIENT(IS_PLAYER(it) && !IS_DEAD(it)) (live participants only), then assigns hunters via FOREACH_CLIENT_RANDOM over the same live set. The port's AssignRoles uses roster.Count (all entries, including dead players and any observers in the list) for the total fed to HunterCount, and marks roster[0..hunters) in deterministic ROSTER ORDER as hunters (its own comment admits 'in roster order'). Consequences for the T53 hunter-disclosure set: (a) the hunter FRACTION is taken over a possibly-larger denominator, so the number of hunters disclosed at round end can differ from stock; (b) hunters are always the first roster slots rather than a random subset, which is also a fairness/gameplay divergence. The bound(1, .., total-1) clamp is faithful. This is role-assignment fidelity rather than a wire bug, but it changes the CONTENT the (correct) wire carries.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In AssignRoles, count only live players (`!IsDead`) for the hunter total and select the hunter subset randomly (Prandom shuffle / FOREACH_CLIENT_RANDOM analogue) over live players, matching Surv_RoundStart.

verdict.title: Survival hunter assignment uses full roster + deterministic order instead of live-player count + random pick
verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED against Base. Base Surv_RoundStart (common/gametypes/gametype/survival/sv_survival.qc:163-183): (1) playercount is incremented ONLY for FOREACH_CLIENT(IS_PLAYER(it) && !IS_DEAD(it)) — live, non-observer players (lines 164-174); (2) hunter_count = bound(1, floor(playercount*0.25), playercount-1) over that live count (line 175); (3) hunters are assigned via FOREACH_CLIENT_RANDOM(IS_PLAYER && !IS_DEAD) marking the first hunter_count of the shuffled set (lines 177-183). I verified FOREACH_CLIENT_RANDOM in server/utils.qh:54-79 — it is a genuine random() Fisher-Yates shuffle, so stock hunters are a RANDOM subset of live players.

Port AssignRoles (src/XonoticGodot.Common/Gameplay/GameTypes/Survival.cs:185-202): total = roster.Count (line 187), and hunters are marked on roster[0..hunters) in fixed index order (lines 200-201, with comment "in roster order"). HunterCount (205-213) ports bound(1,...,total-1) faithfully, but feeds it the wrong denominator.

I traced the roster: GameWorld.cs:1441 passes Clients.Players, which is ClientManager._players — documented at ClientManager.cs:64 as "connected clients (alive or awaiting respawn)" and it includes observers/dead. The class even has a separate LivePlayers() filter (ClientManager.cs:585-597, excludes IsDead and IsObserver) that AssignRoles does NOT use. So two real divergences vs Base: (a) the hunter denominator includes dead/observer entries → a different hunter count than stock when such entries exist (e.g. 8 live + 4 obs: Base floor(8*.25)=2 vs port floor(12*.25)=3); (b) hunters are always the first roster slots rather than a random subset — predictable in a hidden-role mode, a fairness divergence. Both change the CONTENT the (correct) T53 disclosure wire carries (how many hunters, and which players).

Severity minor: gameplay/fairness fidelity in a niche round mode, not a crash or wire break; materially diverges only when observers/dead are in the roster or when players exploit the fixed first-slot assignment. Suggested fix is sound: filter roster to !IsDead (and exclude observers, matching IS_PLAYER) before counting, and pick the hunter subset via a random shuffle.

confirmed.title: Movevar decode silently ignores a configured/replicated 0 for any field whose port default is non-zero (stepdown, airstrafeaccel_qw, disable_on_ramps) → diverges from Base on every non-Xonotic physics config
confirmed.baseRef: Base/data/xonotic-data.pk3dir/physics.cfg:91/131/171/251/291/331/371 (g_physics_<set>_stepdown 0), :58/98/138/218/258/298/338 (airstrafeaccel_qw 0); physicsCPMA.cfg:49,70 (global sv_airstrafeaccel_qw 0 / sv_gameplayfix_stepdown 0); common/physics/player.qc:54-64,134-148 Physics_ClientOption returns the cvar value verbatim (a real 0 is a real 0)
confirmed.detail: The decode helpers Raw(fallback)=`(x==0 && fallback!=0)?fallback:x`, B(fallback)=`x!=0 || fallback`, and their FromCvars twins CvarRaw/CvarBool, treat a value of 0 as "cvar unset → use Xonotic default". But many real configs deliberately set these to 0. sv_gameplayfix_stepdown (port default StepDown=2) is set to 0 by EVERY non-Xonotic preset (nexuiz/quake/warsow/quake3/cpma/vecxis/quake2) and by physicsCPMA.cfg globally; sv_airstrafeaccel_qw (port default AirStrafeAccelQW=-0.95) is set to 0 by quake/warsow/quake3/cpma/vecxis/quake2/bones. When such a config is in play, MoveVarsBlock.Apply stamps "0" but FromCvars reads it back as the non-zero default (CvarRaw("sv_gameplayfix_stepdown",2)→2); the v7 preset path (FromValues) does the same. Base QC uses the real 0. Net: stair-stepping (StepDown) and air-strafe accel diverge from Base on any non-default physics. Client and server agree with EACH OTHER (so no rubber-banding), but both are wrong vs the spec — a player on a CPMA/Nexuiz/Quake3 server gets Xonotic stepdown+airstrafe behavior. Stock Xonotic config keeps all these at their non-zero/true defaults, so golden movement traces are unaffected.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Distinguish 'cvar present' from 'value 0'. For the wire path, FromValues already receives a positional vector where every slot is sent — drop the Raw/B 'fall back on 0' logic for fields that are always present on the wire and assign verbatim (the wire's 0 is authoritative). For FromCvars, gate the fallback on cvar EXISTENCE (Api.Cvars.Has / GetString().Length!=0) rather than value==0, matching DP's CVAR_TYPEFLAG_EXISTS semantics used elsewhere (PhysicsPreset.Exists). Add a decode test that round-trips a config with sv_gameplayfix_stepdown 0 and sv_airstrafeaccel_qw 0 and asserts StepDown==0 / AirStrafeAccelQW==0.

verdict.title: Movevar decode clobbers an authoritative 0/false back to the Xonotic default (stepdown, airstrafeaccel_qw, disable_on_ramps) → diverges from Base on non-default physics configs
verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed from both sides.

BASE: Base/.../qcsrc/common/physics/player.qc:23-42 Physics_ClientOption returns cvar(s) verbatim once the cvar EXISTS (CVAR_TYPEFLAG_EXISTS) — a real 0 is authoritative, for both the preset lookup and the autocvar_sv_* fallback. physics.cfg confirms deliberate zeros: 7 presets set stepdown 0 (nexuiz/quake/warsow/quake3/cpma/vecxis/quake2), 8 set airstrafeaccel_qw 0 (those + bones), 6 set jumpspeedcap_max_disable_on_ramps 0. physicsCPMA.cfg:49,70 sets the GLOBAL sv_airstrafeaccel_qw 0 and sv_gameplayfix_stepdown 0.

PORT: MovementParameters.cs decode helpers destroy an authoritative 0/false whenever the port default is non-zero/true:
- FromValues: Raw(fallback)=`(x==0 && fallback!=0)?fallback:x` (:339), B(fallback)=`x!=0||fallback` (:340). StepDown=(int)Raw(2) at :369, AirStrafeAccelQW=Raw(-0.95) at :348, JumpSpeedCapMaxDisableOnRamps=B(true) at :364. A wire 0/false → snaps to 2 / -0.95 / true.
- FromCvars twins CvarRaw (:395-401), CvarBool (:403-408) do the same on live cvars: CvarRaw("sv_gameplayfix_stepdown",2) on a real 0 → 2; CvarRaw("sv_airstrafeaccel_qw",-0.95) → -0.95; CvarBool("...disable_on_ramps",true) → true.

REACHABILITY confirmed end-to-end: PhysicsPreset.Resolve (PhysicsPreset.cs:110-132) returns PresetFloat = the real preset 0; MoveVarsBlock.CaptureResolved/Capture build a full-length vector with that 0; FromValues then clobbers it. The global path needs no preset: a server execing physicsCPMA.cfg makes global sv_gameplayfix_stepdown genuinely 0, which FromCvars (server) and Apply→FromCvars (client) both read back as 2.

BEHAVIOR diverges: PlayerPhysics.cs:1180 `StepDown==0` skips the step-down gluing block; :494 `AirStrafeAccelQW != 0f` selects an air-accel branch; :833 disable_on_ramps gates the jumpspeedcap. So a CPMA/Nexuiz/Quake3 config gets Xonotic stepdown + airstrafe behavior instead of the spec.

The reviewer's fix is sound: Capture/CaptureResolved always emit every slot, so a present wire 0 is authoritative — FromValues should assign verbatim (the short-vector/older-peer tail is already handled by the separate `i < v.Length` guard, so dropping Raw/B there loses nothing), and FromCvars should gate the fallback on cvar EXISTENCE not value==0 (matching PhysicsPreset.Exists / DP CVAR_TYPEFLAG_EXISTS).

Severity major, not blocker: client and server compute the SAME wrong value, so there is no rubber-banding/desync — it is a silent spec divergence that only appears on non-Xonotic physics configs; stock Xonotic keeps all these at their non-zero/true defaults, so golden movement traces are unaffected.

confirmed.title: Wrong default: Defaults.JumpSpeedCapMaxDisableOnRamps = true, but stock Xonotic sets sv_jumpspeedcap_max_disable_on_ramps 0
confirmed.baseRef: Base/data/xonotic-data.pk3dir/xonotic-server.cfg:94 `set sv_jumpspeedcap_max_disable_on_ramps 0`; common/physics/player.qh:48 `bool autocvar_sv_jumpspeedcap_max_disable_on_ramps;` (no initializer → 0); player.qc:144,452 consumer
confirmed.detail: The QC autocvar has no initializer (defaults false) and stock xonotic-server.cfg explicitly sets it to 0. The port's Defaults sets it true and the field comment wrongly annotates "(1)". Consumer PlayerPhysics.cs:833 `onRamp = ... && mp.JumpSpeedCapMaxDisableOnRamps` — when true the upper jumpspeedcap is disabled on ramps. In STOCK config sv_jumpspeedcap_max is NaN so the whole branch (player.qc:448, PlayerPhysics.cs:826) is skipped and golden traces are unaffected — hence minor. But it bites two ways once a finite cap exists: (a) the xdf preset sets jumpspeedcap_max 0.5 WITH disable_on_ramps 1 and quake3 sets jumpspeedcap_max 0 WITH disable_on_ramps 0 — for quake3 the port default true is wrong; (b) it compounds with the B()-helper bug above (a replicated disable_on_ramps 0 is ignored because B(true) forces true), so the value can never be corrected over the wire.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Set Defaults.JumpSpeedCapMaxDisableOnRamps = false and fix the :60 comment to "(0)". (Also fixed transitively by the B()/CvarBool existence-gate fix in the major finding, which would then let a replicated/configured value override the default in both directions.)

verdict.title: Defaults.JumpSpeedCapMaxDisableOnRamps = true is the correct stock-Xonotic value (not a wrong default)
verdict.reasoning: Refuted. The reviewer concluded "stock = 0" from two sources but missed the load order. xonotic-server.cfg execs BOTH physicsX.cfg (line 675) and physics.cfg (line 682), and the literal `set sv_jumpspeedcap_max_disable_on_ramps 0` is on line 94 — i.e. it runs BEFORE the physicsX.cfg exec. physicsX.cfg is labeled "current Xonotic physics" (physicsX.cfg:1-2) and does a bare assignment `sv_jumpspeedcap_max_disable_on_ramps 1` (physicsX.cfg:55). Because that exec runs after line 94, the effective live value of the cvar in stock Xonotic is 1, not 0. The line-94 `set` only declares/seeds the cvar; the preset exec overrides it. This is corroborated by physics.cfg:49 `set g_physics_xonotic_jumpspeedcap_max_disable_on_ramps 1` — the g_physics_xonotic_* namespaced block, which the port header explicitly cites as the source of its Defaults (MovementParameters.cs:14-15, 184-186), also says 1. So Defaults.JumpSpeedCapMaxDisableOnRamps = true (line 224) and the comment "(1)" (line 60) are both correct and faithful to stock Xonotic; changing them to false/"(0)" would introduce a divergence. The QC autocvar having no initializer (player.qh:48) is irrelevant because the engine always loads a physics preset that assigns the value. The reviewer's "bites two ways" arguments (quake3 preset, B()-helper compounding) are real concerns but belong entirely to the separate CvarBool/B() existence-gate finding the reviewer cross-references — they are about the read/replication helpers wrongly forcing true for a live/wire 0, NOT about the default constant. The default constant under review is correct. Consumer chain confirmed: PlayerPhysics reads mp.JumpSpeedCapMaxDisableOnRamps; QC player.qc:447-458 gates the whole branch behind !isnan(JUMPSPEEDCAP_MAX), and stock max is "nan" (physicsX.cfg:54), so in stock play the value is inert anyway — but where it does matter, true is the correct stock value.

confirmed.title: Weapon registry sorted FULLY alphabetically; QC keeps the first 19 weapons in definition order — weapon_byid_N selects the wrong weapon
confirmed.baseRef: common/weapons/all.qh:353 REGISTRY_SORT(Weapons, WEP_HARDCODED_IMPULSES + 1) + lib/registry.qh:166-198 + common/weapons/all.inc:1-30 + server/impulse.qc:150-172 + common/weapons/all.qh:356-382
confirmed.detail: QC sorts the weapon registry with skip = WEP_HARDCODED_IMPULSES+1 = 20: the heapsort runs over (COUNT - 20) entries, so registry index 0 (WEP_Null) and indices 1..19 (the 19 core weapons) KEEP DEFINITION ORDER (blaster=1, shotgun=2, machinegun=3, mortar=4, minelayer=5, electro=6, crylink=7, vortex=8, hagar=9, devastator=10, porto=11, vaporizer=12, hook=13, hlac=14, tuba=15, rifle=16, fireball=17, seeker=18, arc=19). Only weapons from index 20+ (Overkill etc.) are strcmp-sorted. The whole point (all.inc:1-3 'ONLY EVER ADD NEW WEAPONS AT THE END ... TO AVOID RENUMBERING OF WEAPON IMPULSES') is stable impulse numbers. QC then assigns m_unique_impulse starting at 230 in this mostly-definition order, skipping WEP_Null, so weapon_byid_0 (impulse 230) = blaster, _1 = shotgun, etc. The port's Registry<Weapon>.Sort() sorts ALL weapons by NetName ordinal (verified in the emitted GeneratedRegistrations.g.cs: Arc, Blaster, Crylink, Devastator, ... then Sort() keeps that order), so Registry<Weapon>.ById(0) = Arc. ByIdHandle maps idx -> ById(idx) directly, so weapon_byid_0 / impulse 230 selects ARC, not blaster — the entire direct-weapon-by-id selection is scrambled vs stock. This is reachable: ServerNet.cs:773 routes `impulse N` -> Commands.cs:1067 WeaponImpulses.Handle -> ByIdHandle. NetGame.WeaponCommandToImpulse:1903-1905 maps weapon_byid_N -> 230+N. (Mitigation: the default number-key path uses weapon_group_N driven by w.Impulse (Blaster.Impulse=1 etc., which DO match QC), so groups 1-9 are unaffected; only by-id binds are wrong. The WeaponImpulses.cs:127-132 comment explicitly but incorrectly claims this is 'the same registry-order assignment QC uses'.)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Make Registry<Weapon>.Sort preserve a stable prefix the way QC does: keep the core 19 in registration/definition order (or assign m_unique_impulse from a QC-faithful weapon-order table) and only ordinal-sort the tail. Equivalently, give Weapon an explicit unique-impulse/order field seeded from the QC WEP order rather than deriving by-id from the alphabetical RegistryId. Then ByIdHandle should look up by that field (skipping a Null/specialattack/mutatorblocked, like all.qh:356-382), not Registry<Weapon>.ById(idx).

verdict.title: Weapon registry sorted fully alphabetically; QC keeps the first 19 in definition order, so weapon_byid_N selects the wrong weapon
verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED — verified on both sides.

QC side (Base): all.qh:353 `REGISTRY_SORT(Weapons, WEP_HARDCODED_IMPULSES + 1)` with `WEP_HARDCODED_IMPULSES = 19` (all.inc:30) ⇒ skip = 20. registry.qh:166-198 (REGISTRY_SORT_2): the swap/cmp functions add `skip` to both indices and the STATIC_INIT runs `heapsort(id_COUNT - skip, ...)`, so only registry indices [20, COUNT) are strcmp-sorted; index 0 (WEP_Null) and 1..19 (the 19 core weapons, all.inc:6-26: blaster, shotgun, machinegun, mortar, minelayer, electro, crylink, vortex, hagar, devastator, porto, vaporizer, hook, hlac, tuba, rifle, fireball, seeker, arc) KEEP DEFINITION ORDER. all.qh:356-382 (register_weapons_done) then assigns `m_unique_impulse` from WEP_IMPULSE_BEGIN=230 in FOREACH order, skipping Null/SPECIALATTACK/(MUTATORBLOCKED&TYPE_OTHER), so impulse 230 = blaster. impulses/all.qh:88-89 maps `weapon_byid_0`→impulse 230; impulse.qc:150-165 `weapon_byid_handle` does `Weapon_from_impulse(230)` (all.qh:150-154, lookup by m_unique_impulse) = blaster. So QC weapon_byid_0=blaster, _1=shotgun, … _18=arc.

Port side: Registry.cs:50-54 `Sort()` orders ALL entries by ordinal RegistryName; GameplayBases.cs:47 `RegistryName => NetName`; NetNames are lowercase (arc, blaster, crylink, …) so ById(0)=arc. RegistryGenerator.cs:242-250 emits `Registry<Weapon>.Sort()` after registering, and there is NO Null weapon in the port (Weapons/ dir has no NullWeapon). WeaponImpulses.cs:134-138 `ByIdHandle(actor, idx)` → `Registry<Weapon>.ById(idx)` directly, so weapon_byid_0 selects ARC, not blaster. The WeaponImpulses.cs:130-131 comment ("the same registry-order assignment QC uses") is factually wrong — QC does NOT alphabetize the core 19.

Reachability confirmed: ServerNet.cs:773 dispatches `impulse {cmd.Impulse}` → Commands → WeaponImpulses.Handle (line 91-93: imp∈[230,253] → ByIdHandle(imp-230)); NetGame.cs:1903-1905 maps weapon_byid_N → 230+N. No test pins the by-id→weapon mapping (InputCommandImpulseTests only checks wire round-trip of the impulse byte; MenuWeaponOrderTests is unrelated).

Net: every direct weapon_byid_N bind selects the wrong weapon vs stock, and the per-weapon unique-impulse ordering (also what CSQC's generated `alias weapon_<netname>` aliases and accuracy/stats indexing key off) is scrambled.

Mitigation (lowers exposure, not validity): the default number-key path uses weapon_group_N driven by Weapon.Impulse (Blaster.Impulse=1 etc., which DO match QC), so the stock 1-9 keyboard layout is unaffected — only explicit weapon_byid_* binds are wrong, and those are not the default layout. That limited default-path exposure is why this is major rather than blocker. Suggested fix is sound: give Weapon a QC-faithful unique-impulse/order field (skip Null/specialattack/mutatorblocked) and have ByIdHandle look up by it, rather than deriving by-id from the alphabetical RegistryId.

confirmed.title: SourceGenTests parity test enshrines the alphabetical weapon order as 'expected' — cannot catch the by-id divergence
confirmed.portLocation: tests/XonoticGodot.Tests/SourceGenTests.cs:283-293 (compares Weapons.All order against a SortedSet<string> from ReflectionScan)
confirmed.baseRef: common/weapons/all.qh:353 (REGISTRY_SORT skip=20) — the canonical weapon order the wire/impulses depend on
confirmed.detail: Bootstrap_GeneratedRegistration_MatchesIndependentReflectionScan compares the live registry order against expected['Weapon'] which is a SortedSet<string> (ordinal). Both sides are alphabetical, so this is a self-consistent round-trip of two PORT mechanisms (generator vs reflection) and never checks against QC's definition-order-preserving sort. The test therefore passes while pinning a weapon order that diverges from Base (finding above). The count-floor tripwires (304-311) and content-hash checks (297-300) are good and not tautologies, but no test guards the QC weapon-impulse mapping. Same blind spot in MenuWeaponOrderTests (round-trips of the port's own RegistryId).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a test pinning weapon_byid_0..N -> {blaster, shotgun, machinegun, mortar, ...} (the QC m_unique_impulse order) and assert Weapons.ById(0).NetName == 'blaster'. This will fail today and is the regression guard for the fix above.

verdict.title: SourceGenTests weapon-order assertion is a tautological round-trip and no test pins the by-id weapon mapping
verdict.reasoning:
All of the finding's factual claims hold up against the code I read on both sides.

PORT side: tests/XonoticGodot.Tests/SourceGenTests.cs line 283 builds `expected` via `ReflectionScan()`, whose buckets are `SortedSet<string>(StringComparer.Ordinal)` (lines 230-236) — i.e. ordinal-alphabetical. Line 287 then does `Assert.Equal(expected["Weapon"], Weapons.All.Select(x => x.RegistryName).ToList())`. The live `Weapons.All` order comes from `Registry<T>.Sort()` (src/.../Framework/Registry.cs:50-54), which is `_all.Sort(string.CompareOrdinal(a.RegistryName, b.RegistryName))` — also ordinal-alphabetical. `RegistryName => NetName` (GameplayBases.cs:47), so both sides are the SAME alphabetical sequence. The assertion is therefore a self-consistent round-trip of two port mechanisms and cannot catch a by-id ordering divergence. The count floors (304-311) and content-hash checks (297-300) are independent and fine, as the reviewer notes.

The underlying divergence is REAL: QC `common/weapons/all.qh:353` is `REGISTRY_SORT(Weapons, WEP_HARDCODED_IMPULSES + 1)` with `WEP_HARDCODED_IMPULSES 19` (all.inc:30). REGISTRY_SORT (lib/registry.qh:166-198) heapsorts by `registered_id` strcmp but SKIPS the first 20 entries, so QC's canonical order is the first 19 weapons in DEFINITION order (blaster, shotgun, machinegun, mortar, minelayer, electro, crylink, vortex, hagar, devastator, porto... per all.inc) followed by the rest alphabetically. `register_weapons_done` then assigns `m_id`/`m_unique_impulse` over that order, and the `weapon_byid_N` config aliases come from `it.netname` in that order. The port sorts ALL weapons alphabetically (arc, blaster, crylink, ...), so `weapon_byid_0` (impulse 230) selects arc in the port vs blaster in stock. WeaponImpulses.cs:134-138 (`ByIdHandle`) maps the by-id index straight to `Registry<Weapon>.ById(idx)`, and its comment claims this is "the same registry-order assignment QC uses for the unique impulses" — which is false.

No test guards this: searched tests/ and found only round-trips of the port's own RegistryId (MenuWeaponOrderTests, CvarReplicationTests using `ByName("blaster").RegistryId`) and generic `Weapons.All[0]` usage — exactly the blind spot described.

NUANCE / why minor not major: this is a test-COVERAGE gap, not a runtime fault. The port made a DELIBERATE, documented divergence (WeaponOrder.cs:23-28 "PORT DIVERGENCE (registry id space)"; WeaponImpulses.cs:131 "The port doesn't model m_unique_impulse and registers no Null weapon"). The port is internally self-consistent (its client and server are both the port; it does not interop with stock QC), so by-id selection works within the port. The reviewer's specific suggested assertion `Weapons.ById(0).NetName == "blaster"` actually presumes parity with stock and would contradict the port's chosen alphabetical ordering — so a regression guard should pin the port's INTENDED by-id order explicitly (whatever the project decides that is), rather than blindly assert "blaster". The valuable, defensible core of the finding stands: the existing assertion is tautological w.r.t. order and nothing pins the by-id weapon mapping, so it could silently drift. Confirmed, severity minor.

confirmed.title: Zombie melee damage/delay are hardcoded fields, not read from g_monster_zombie_attack_melee_* cvars (live-tuning fidelity gap)
confirmed.portLocation: src/XonoticGodot.Common/Gameplay/Monsters/Zombie.cs:22-23 (MeleeDamage=55f, MeleeDelay=1f fields) used directly at :105 MeleeAttack
confirmed.baseRef: monsters.cfg:6-7 (g_monster_zombie_attack_melee_damage 55, _delay 1) read live by zombie.qc attack
confirmed.detail: Unlike speed_walk/run/stop and damageforcescale (which go through MonsterAI.Cvar at Spawn/Think, Zombie.cs:48-51,79), MeleeDamage and MeleeDelay are plain fields consumed directly in Attack(). The DEFAULT values match Base (55/1 — the canary is correct), so the T31 canary passes, but a server changing g_monster_zombie_attack_melee_damage via cfg/vote has no effect on melee. Minor (default-correct) but a silent live-cvar divergence; same pattern likely in other monster descriptors (LeapDamage/LeapForce/etc. are also fields).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Read MeleeDamage/MeleeDelay (and the leap params) via MonsterAI.Cvar('g_monster_zombie_attack_melee_damage', 55f) at attack/spawn time, matching how the speeds are handled, so cfg/vote changes take effect.

verdict.title: Zombie melee/leap damage and delay are static fields, not read live from g_monster_zombie_attack_* cvars (live-tuning fidelity gap)
verdict.reasoning: Confirmed against both sides. Base QC (Base/.../monsters/monster/zombie.qc, M_Zombie_Attack) reads the cvars LIVE at attack time: Monster_Attack_Melee(..., autocvar_g_monster_zombie_attack_melee_damage, ..., autocvar_g_monster_zombie_attack_melee_delay, ...) and Monster_Attack_Leap(..., autocvar_g_monster_zombie_attack_leap_speed..., autocvar_g_monster_zombie_attack_leap_delay) with leap_damage/leap_force read in M_Zombie_Attack_Leap_Touch. autocvar_* in QC re-reads the cvar each evaluation, so a cfg/vote change takes effect immediately. The port (src/XonoticGodot.Common/Gameplay/Monsters/Zombie.cs:22-30) stores MeleeDamage/MeleeDelay/LeapDamage/LeapForce/LeapSpeed/LeapDelay as plain fields set once in the constructor and consumes them directly at :105 (MeleeAttack) and :112-113,129 (leap) — they are never refreshed from MonsterAI.Cvar. The infrastructure exists and is used right beside them: WalkSpeed/RunSpeed/StopSpeed/DamageForceScale/Loot go through MonsterAI.Cvar at Spawn (:48-52) and damageforcescale is re-read in Think (:79); MonsterAI.Cvar (MonsterAI.cs:1621) reads Api.Cvars.GetFloat live each call. Defaults all match monsters.cfg:2-7 (55/1/60/55/500/1.5), so the T31 canary passes — this is purely a silent live-cvar divergence, not a wrong default. The reviewer also correctly notes the same pattern across other monsters (Mage SpikeDamage/PushDamage, Spider BiteDamage are static fields too), so the port systematically captures attack damage at construction rather than reading it live. Real divergence from Base, but low impact: monsters are an opt-in mode and mid-match voting of melee damage is rare, hence minor (default-correct).
